title: Standard Precautions and Hand Hygiene
subtitle: Across a multi-patient assignment, the nurse applies standard precautions to every patient — hand hygiene, the right PPE for the anticipated exposure, and safe sharps practices — and knows when soap and water are required over an alcohol rub.

patient.name: Standard Precautions
patient.admitDate: The nurse applies standard precautions to every patient: hand hygiene, PPE matched to exposure, and safe sharps handling.

vitals.temp: No recapping; sharps box at point of use

nursesNote: The nurse applies standard precautions to every patient regardless of diagnosis, treating all blood, body fluids, secretions, excretions (except sweat), non-intact skin, and mucous membranes as potentially infectious. Hand hygiene is performed before and after patient contact, before an aseptic task, and after exposure to body fluids or the patient's surroundings. An alcohol-based hand rub is preferred when hands are not visibly soiled; soap and water are used when hands are soiled or when caring for a patient with C. difficile or norovirus (spores/viruses resist alcohol). PPE is selected for the anticipated exposure: gloves for body fluids or non-intact skin, a gown if clothing may be soiled, and a mask with eye protection when splashes or sprays are likely. Gloves never replace hand hygiene. Safe injection and sharps practices apply — never recap needles two-handed, and dispose of sharps in a puncture-resistant container at the point of use. PPE is removed in order (gloves, goggles, gown, mask) followed by hand hygiene.
providerOrders: Apply standard precautions to every patient, every time
Perform hand hygiene before and after patient contact and aseptic tasks
Use soap and water for visibly soiled hands, C. difficile, or norovirus
Select PPE based on the anticipated exposure to body fluids
Never recap needles two-handed; dispose of sharps at the point of use

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about standard precautions?

opts.text: Standard precautions apply to ALL patients regardless of diagnosis
Treat all blood and body fluids as potentially infectious
They are used only for patients known to have an infection
Wear gloves when contact with body fluids or non-intact skin is expected
Perform hand hygiene before and after patient contact
Sweat is considered highly infectious under standard precautions

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What guides hand hygiene and PPE?

opts.text: Alcohol-based hand rub is preferred when hands are not visibly soiled
Soap and water are required for C. difficile or visibly soiled hands
Choose PPE based on the anticipated exposure to body fluids
Recapping needles two-handed is a safe sharps practice
Wearing gloves replaces the need for hand hygiene
A mask and eye protection are worn when splashes or sprays are likely

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order donning PPE before a procedure with splash risk

opts.text: Perform hand hygiene
Put on the gown
Put on the mask and eye protection
Put on gloves over the gown cuffs
Provide care, then remove PPE and perform hand hygiene

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Soap and water, or is an alcohol rub okay?

opts.text: Hands are visibly soiled with blood
Caring for a patient with C. difficile
Routine cleansing between tasks, hands not soiled
After caring for a patient with norovirus
Cleaning hands before donning gloves, hands clean
Hands feel sticky or contaminated
Quick decontamination after touching a bed rail, hands clean
After using the restroom

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Sequence removing soiled PPE after a procedure
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Remove gloves (most contaminated)
Remove goggles or face shield by the band
Remove the gown, rolling it inward
Remove the mask by the ties without touching the front
Perform hand hygiene
Performing hand hygiene before and after every patient contact
Reusing the same pair of gloves between two patients
Using soap and water for a patient with C. difficile
Recapping a used needle with two hands
Wearing eye protection when a splash is anticipated
Skipping hand hygiene because gloves were worn
Disposing of sharps in a puncture-resistant container at point of use
Treating standard precautions as optional for 'clean' patients

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Safety and Infection Control, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
